reset_biology_synthesis.recommendation: Reset Biology suggests asking the practitioner to consider progesterone as the first hormone-replacement building block for women presenting with anxiety, sleep disruption, or PMS — Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:04:57.110): "I would say the first building block would be the progesterone" and Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:06:46.160): "a woman that is in their 30s, I think progesterone every day can be extremely beneficial." We'd ask the prescribing physician to weigh dose by life-stage (premenopausal, perimenopausal, postmenopausal) and to titrate to symptom relief — Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:19:14.720): "dosing at 200 milligrams" and (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:19:17.350): "it's really designed on symptom dosing." Trevor frames the mechanism — Trevor (hFrAZsG5D1s 00:23:31.039): "Progesterone is your natural GABA agonist. Yes, it is. It calms the brain like a benzo a benzoizamine."
reset_biology_synthesis.caveats:
Dose titration vs symptom: Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:19:17.350): "it's really designed on symptom dosing. So you really want a symptom relief when dosing progesterone"; Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:07:25.520): "It's always better to start lower and work your way up."
Over-dosing signal — short menstrual cycles: Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:18:45.280): "that would be a side effect of my progesterone being dosed too high is that I end up getting very short menstrual cycles."
Synthetic progestins ≠ bioidentical progesterone: Hunter email threadId 19596b0f5e9e1e6f (2025-03-14): "Bioidentical hormones (estradiol, progesterone, testosterone) work better and are safer than the synthetic versions used in the WHI study"; Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:08:17.919): synthetic progestins keep "a woman locked in her lure phase of her menstrual cycle, which is what we known as the PMS phase."
Acne can flare with progesterone: Taylor (YCSzE066krM 00:03:04.959): "some of the things that I take such as like progesterone that can also cause acne to um appear."
Trevor's contrarian framing — menopause is metabolic first: Trevor (hFrAZsG5D1s 00:00:08.960): "Menopause, pmenopause. How it's not an HRT issue. It's not an estrogen issue. It's not a progesterone issue. It's a metabolic issue." Don't substitute progesterone for missing metabolic work (insulin sensitivity, muscle mass, sleep, real food).
Side-effects of deficiency the practitioner should screen for before starting: Taylor (xIVKpRqZXP4 00:05:52.280): "one of the side effects of low progesterone is not being able to stay asleep at night"; Taylor (xIVKpRqZXP4 00:02:22.200): "that is going to be one of the first signs that you are progesterone deficient and trust me it's not fun it is not natural to miss your periods."
Some women genuinely don't tolerate it — Taylor (IW0-bllx4dk 01:39:35.600): "there are a lot of women that don't do well with progesterone."
reset_biology_synthesis.cross_expert_consensus: All three experts agree progesterone supports women's sleep, mood, and anxiety. Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:06:46.160): "a woman that is in their 30s, I think progesterone every day can be extremely beneficial, especially if you're a woman who has anxiety." Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:17:01.600): "this can really be adjusted based on symptoms that they're having, which is symptoms of anxiety, sleep issues, bleeding, hot flashes, you know, heavy menstrual cycle bleedings." Trevor (hFrAZsG5D1s 00:23:31.039): "Progesterone is your natural GABA agonist. Yes, it is. It calms the brain like a benzo a benzoizamine, right? Valium without the hangover." Mechanism agreement: Trevor (hbo13qmxx-k 00:06:02.880): "Estrogen and progesterone are the primary modulators of something called GABA, which is your brain's chief inhibitory neurotransmitter" and Trevor (cOoT-pGvrEU 00:29:23.440): "estrogen and progesterone regulate gabaurgic and glutamatergic balance in your brain." Bioidentical-over-synthetic agreement: Hunter email threadId 19596b0f5e9e1e6f (2025-03-14): "Bioidentical hormones (estradiol, progesterone, testosterone) work better and are safer than the synthetic versions used in the WHI study"; Taylor (PvvfZxiFfjQ 00:10:50.480): "I also take 200 milligrams of progesterone capsules every night."
reset_biology_synthesis.cross_expert_disagreement: Daily-vs-luteal-only timing — Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:06:05.360): "premenopausal, I would say anywhere between 100 to 200 milligrams. Most women are going to do days 14 through 28 of the menstrual cycle" then (00:06:46.160) "a woman that is in their 30s, I think progesterone every day can be extremely beneficial." Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:16:42.320): "women anywhere between 100 to 200 milligrams on days 14 through 28. Now I do know some women and me personally I take progesterone every day." Trevor doesn't endorse routine daily HRT progesterone — Trevor (hFrAZsG5D1s 00:00:08.960): "Menopause, pmenopause. How it's not an HRT issue. It's not an estrogen issue. It's not a progesterone issue. It's a metabolic issue." Dose by life-stage — Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:17:01.600) perimenopause range "between 100 to 300" milligrams; Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:17:17.350) post-menopausal "go on anywhere between 200 to 600 milligrams nightly"; Hunter (JgktB39WB_A 00:06:05.360) "between 100 to 200 milligrams" for premenopausal — keep both ranges, don't average. Route — Hunter (0TyW-wlJMxo 00:32:28.960): "the transdermal was more effective than the oral, probably. I think that's the future"; Taylor's lived practice (PvvfZxiFfjQ 00:10:50.480): "200 milligrams of progesterone capsules every night" (oral). Newer Hunter overrides older route preference but cite both — Hunter email threadId 19596b0f5e9e1e6f (2025-03-14): "Transdermal or injectable estrogen is safer than oral estrogen. It avoids liver metabolism and reduces clot risk" (this email is about estrogen route logic, not progesterone — surface for context, not as a progesterone-specific instruction).

reset_biology_synthesis.female_specific_notes: Taylor's lived protocol — Taylor (PvvfZxiFfjQ 00:10:50.480): "I also take 200 milligrams of progesterone capsules every night." Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:16:42.320): "women anywhere between 100 to 200 milligrams on days 14 through 28. Now I do know some women and me personally I take progesterone every day." Life-stage dose ladder per Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:17:01.600): premenopausal cyclic days 14–28 "100 to 200" mg; perimenopausal "between 100 to 300" titrated to symptoms; (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:17:17.350) postmenopausal "between 200 to 600 milligrams nightly." Symptom screen the practitioner should run before starting — Taylor (xIVKpRqZXP4 00:05:52.280): "one of the side effects of low progesterone is not being able to stay asleep at night"; Taylor (xIVKpRqZXP4 00:02:22.200): "that is going to be one of the first signs that you are progesterone deficient and trust me it's not fun it is not natural to miss your periods." Symptom-relief targets per Taylor (V87f6GRxZLM 00:25:54.080): "100 200 milligrams this is going to be this is like a range of what I take you know this is going to be different for each person" and "to help reduce the anxiety at night um and sleeping and just overall mood balance." Benefits per Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:19:35.679): "breast and ovarian protection, reducing PMS" and (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:19:43.200): "reducing ovarian cysts. It's going to help with miscarriages. It's also going to reduce postpartum depression." Over-dosing signal in cycling women — Taylor (ftHGXMFuiJs 00:18:45.280): "a side effect of my progesterone being dosed too high is that I end up getting very short menstrual cycles" — relevant when fertility is the goal. Tolerance variability — Taylor (IW0-bllx4dk 01:39:35.600): "there are a lot of women that don't do well with progesterone" so the practitioner should be ready to back off, not push through.
